title: Sex- and gender-based analysis plus: Public Health Agency of Canada's 2024-25 Departmental Results Report - Canada.ca
content:
## Sex- and gender-based analysis plus: Public Health Agency of Canada's 2024-25 Departmental Results Report
- Section 1: Institutional SGBA Plus governance and capacity Governance Capacity
- Section 2: Gender and diversity impacts, by program Core Responsibility 1: Health promotion and chronic disease prevention Core Responsibility 2: Infectious disease prevention and control Core Responsibility 3: Health security Definitions
- Core Responsibility 1: Health promotion and chronic disease prevention
- Core Responsibility 2: Infectious disease prevention and control
- Core Responsibility 3: Health security

## Section 1: Institutional SGBA Plus governance and capacity
PHAC is committed to applying SGBA Plus – an intersectional, analytical approach – to systematically integrate health equity into all its policies, programs, and initiatives.
In 2024–25, PHAC's SGBA Plus Focal Point, alongside program areas, advanced the 2022–25 PHAC SGBA Plus Action Plan Framework and Implementation Strategy in the following priority areas:
- Enhance SGBA Plus accountability in planning, reporting, and decision-making processes;
- Promote and enable the collection and use of disaggregated data and scientific evidence for intersectional analyses;
- Strengthen awareness, understanding, and capacity for SGBA Plus through tailored training and tools; and
- develop strategic partnerships, and collaborations to enhance equity-informed policy and culturally relevant policies and programming.
The PHAC SGBA Plus Champion, alongside the executive-level SGBA Plus Branch Leads, continued to support the systematic integration of SGBA Plus into the organization's functions and programs, affirming the Agency's commitment to health equity. PHAC continued to integrate health equity considerations in decision-making processes by promoting SGBA Plus discussions at various governance committees and demonstrating progress on SGBA Plus commitments through an annual report to the Agency's Executive Committee.
The PHAC SGBA Plus Focal Point continued to implement the Health Portfolio SGBA Plus Policy by advancing the Agency's 2022–2025 SGBA Plus Action Plan Framework and Implementation Strategy through targeted actions in 2024–25:
- Supported executive-level SGBA Plus Branch Leads in developing, implementing, and monitoring nine SGBA Plus Branch Integration Plans to ensure the systematic integration into Branch activities.
- Embedded SGBA Plus measures in Executive Performance Agreements.
- Delivered a robust challenge and support function by providing equity- and evidence-informed advice and embedding inclusive and non-stigmatising language in 42 Cabinet Documents and 142 high visibility public health documents.
- Integrated SGBA Plus in key PHAC scientific frameworks and policy tools, including the PHAC Science Strategy for 2024–25 to 2029–30 , Vision 2030 – Moving data to public health action , PHAC Roadmap 2030, PHAC Anti-Racism in Science Framework and Action Plan, PHAC Scientific Framework for Guidance Development, and PHAC Grants and Contributions Strategic Framework.
The Agency's SGBA Plus Focal Point continued to enhance knowledge, awareness, and capacity by integrating SGBA Plus into the Agency's core functions through training, presentations, tools, and resources tailored to the public health context.
In 2024–25, the Agency's SGBA Plus Focal Point delivered 40 presentations and training sessions to diverse audiences, reaching over 900 participants. To further support the application of SGBA Plus, new communication products were developed and disseminated across various platforms. Additionally, several new tools and resources were developed to strengthen SGBA Plus capacity across the Agency, including:
- The second edition of the Inclusive Language Guide for PHAC employees, which provides guidance on the respectful use of images and constructing equity-oriented narratives.
- An internal "what we heard" report and accompanying infographic, summarizing key themes, opportunities, and promising practices to enhance the collection, use, analysis, and reporting of disaggregated data by socio-demographic and socio-economic factors in surveillance.
- A published article on key considerations for applying intersectionality theory to partner and stakeholder engagement in public health .
- A rapid narrative review titled Integrating Health Equity in Artificial Intelligence for Public Health in Canada .
- An inventory of evidence-informed interventions to reduce health inequalities highlighting actionable strategies to address social determinants of chronic disease risk factors such as unhealthy eating, physical inactivity, smoking, and problematic alcohol use.
- A report on Inequalities in Mental Health, Well-being and Wellness in Canada and case study which applies an intersectional lens to examine social and structural determinants of mental health and includes a data tool featuring over 15 indicators.
- A report titled Rapid Review: An intersectional analysis of the disproportionate health effects of wildfires on populations and communities .
The PHAC SGBA Plus Network, with over 90 representatives from across PHAC's Centres and Directorates, continued to advance and improve the systemic integration of SGBA Plus across PHAC through strengthened expertise from formal and applied learnings.
Human Resources (full-time equivalents) dedicated to SGBA Plus
In 2024–25, approximately seven Full-Time Equivalents (FTEs) were dedicated to SGBA Plus implementation in the Agency:
- Five FTEs within the SGBA Plus Focal Point dedicated to advancing SGBA Plus capacity and practice; and
- The SGBA Plus Champion, executive-level SGBA Plus Branch Leads, and PHAC SGBA Plus Network members' time, amounting to two FTEs in total.

## Section 2: Gender and diversity impacts, by program

### Core Responsibility 1: Health promotion and chronic disease prevention
Program name: Chronic Disease Prevention
Program goals: This Program seeks new solutions for the complex challenge of preventing chronic diseases in Canada. The program works across sectors to leverage partnerships and investments to design, implement and scale interventions that support healthy behaviours and create social and physical environments that enable and promote better health. The program addresses behavioural risk factors for chronic disease, namely physical inactivity, unhealthy eating and tobacco use, among people who face health inequalities and are at greater risk of developing poor health outcomes, including the main chronic diseases (e.g., diabetes, cardiovascular disease, cancer, etc.). The program also aims to improve the mental health of Black Canadians and support collective action on the social and structural determinants of health to reduce health inequities. Ultimately, the Program contributes to improving health equity and preventing chronic diseases through action on the social determinants of health and effective healthy living policies and interventions to advance health outcomes for all.
Target population: All Canadians that face health inequalities and inequities and are at greater risk of chronic disease.
Third group: broadly gender-balanced
Third group: no significant distributional impacts
Second group: no significant intergenerational impacts or impacts on generations between youths and seniors
Specific demographic group(s) to be monitored
Through research, evidence and consultations in the areas of health equity and chronic disease prevention, several priority populations have been identified. This includes Indigenous Peoples, newcomers to Canada, persons with disabilities, 2SLGBTQI+ communities, people living on low-incomes and racialized communities (e.g., Black and South Asian people).
Key program impacts on gender and diversity
Observed results Footnote *

SGBA Plus data collection plan
Healthy Canadians and Communities Fund (HCCF)
Organizations funded through the HCCF must report the number of individuals reached by gender (female, male, or other gender), age groups (children and youth, adults, older adults) and geographical location (the first three digits of postal codes).
Collecting additional sociodemographic factors to support equity-informed analyses such as employment status, education level, income/income range, immigration status and sexual orientation, are optional. However, projects are encouraged to collect this information if appropriate and relevant to support a more comprehensive equity-informed analysis and understanding of health and social inequities affecting project participants. Funded projects are also encouraged to collect disaggregated data on any outcomes related to knowledge, skills, social, physical, and food environments, health behaviours, and health status. Disaggregated outcomes are reported upon availability of data and on a voluntary basis as this was not a program requirement when most funded organizations started their projects. Since 2021–22, newly funded organizations must report this information in a more systemic manner and this reporting requirement is now embedded in contribution agreements.
To strengthen SGBA Plus integration, the HCCF contracted a consultant in 2022–23 to conduct an environmental scan and key informant interviews with funding recipients to identify barriers and best practices in collecting data disaggregated by sociodemographic factors to support an equity-informed analysis. In 2023–24, the consultant presented their findings, including resources and best practices for newly funded recipients. The report also included considerations for the HCCF regarding clear communication of data collection requirements, more flexible reporting templates and better integration of qualitative data in program performance.
In collaboration with PHAC's Health Equity Integration team, the HCCF led a webinar that focused on SGBA Plus in Spring 2024. The webinar provided information on SGBA Plus and described examples of how to integrate SGBA Plus within funded projects. The webinar was an important step to helping HCCF-funded projects gain knowledge and skills of SGBA Plus and including SGBA Plus in project activities.
Promoting Health Equity: Mental Health of Black Canadians Fund
Using a health equity lens is one of the six guiding principles of Promoting Health Equity: Mental Health of Black Canadians (MHBC) Fund. In 2024–25, the MHBC Fund launched three solicitations that required applicants to apply SGBA Plus and integrate equity-informed analysis into project design, implementation and evaluation. All funded projects were required to describe how their project considers health equity for targeted populations and diverse subgroups which may include: youth, older adults, 2SLGBTQI+ individuals, Black Francophone populations, Black women and Black newcomers.
- The MHBC Fund updated its Evaluation Guidance documents to provide a resource for funding recipients to improve their understanding of SGBA Plus. These documents describe the importance of collecting disaggregated, intersectional and race-based socio-demographic data to capture the lived experiences of diverse subgroups.
- Collecting and reporting on this type of data creates a deeper understanding of health equity gaps, amplifies underrepresented voices and documents the success of interventions, their context and the populations they reached. Ultimately, this helps PHAC make informed decisions that improve the design and delivery of culturally adapted and accessible mental health services and could be used to better inform federal program and policy decisions.
Intersectional Action Fund (ISAF)
Equity is a key guiding principle in the ISAF program design. This includes the requirement for all funding recipients to integrate equity into the design and delivery of their funded projects. In 2023–24, ISAF launched a targeted solicitation where program officials provided additional supportive resources to applicants, including an Applicant Resource Guide and an Invitation to Submit a Funding Request checklist, to support capacity in terms of understanding key health equity principles, and how these principles could be integrated throughout project design and delivery.
Equity is a key guiding principle in ISAF's program design. This includes the requirement for all funding recipients to integrate equity into the design and delivery of their funded projects. In 2024–25, ISAF launched an open solicitation where program officials provided additional supportive resources to applicants, including an Applicant Resource Guide, Funding Request Form Guidance Sheet, and the Integrating Health Equity into Funding Proposals: a Guide for Applicants . The ISAF also held applicant webinars to support capacity in terms of understanding key health equity principles, and how these principles could be integrated throughout project design and delivery.
Through its 2024–25 funding opportunity, ISAF supported 12 projects that focused on breaking down systemic barriers to better health. These projects were selected for their strong commitment to health equity, actions that focused on chronic disease prevention and collaboration across sectors.
To better understand how equity is being put into action, ISAF updated its reporting tools to help funding recipients share how they apply SGBA Plus to their projects and which populations they are reaching. This information will help PHAC improve future programming and share valuable insights on equity-driven approaches to improving the social and structural determinants of health.
Program name: Evidence for Health Promotion, and Chronic Disease and Injury Prevention – Canadian Post COVID-19 Condition (CAN-PCC) Guidelines
Program goals: The evidence-based guidelines for post COVID-19 condition (PCC) aim to develop, disseminate and evaluate six comprehensive, evidence-based guidelines on topics covering the full cycle of the condition. Further program objectives include raising awareness about guidelines and promote their uptake as well as ensuring effectiveness of these guidelines on the ultimate beneficiaries.
Target population: The target population for the CAN-PCC guidelines includes all Canadians, with a focus on priority populations such as Indigenous Peoples, refugees and migrants, 2SLGBTQI+ individuals, older adults in need of care, women, pregnant women and people, racialized groups, justice-involved people, people with no fixed housing, and those living in rural and remote communities.
Third group: broadly gender-balanced
Third group: no significant distributional impacts
Second group: no significant intergenerational impacts or impacts on generations between youths and seniors
Specific demographic group(s) to be monitored
When evidence is available, the CAN-PCC collaborative will develop recommendations for equity-deserving groups such as Indigenous Peoples, refugees, migrants, and 2SLGBTQI+ individuals, addressing their unique healthcare needs and barriers for each guideline topic. Different age groups are also considered when developing the recommendations, including separate ones for children.
Key program impacts on gender and diversity
The CAN-PCC guidelines integrate equity considerations throughout the development process, ensuring that gender and diversity impacts are central to the recommendations. This involves prioritizing evidence and relevant information related to equity-deserving groups to inform the drafting of equity considerations for the guidelines of the program. Additionally, an Equity Oversight Committee, comprising representatives of priority populations and community leaders, has been established to support the interpretation of evidence for recommendation development.
Other key program impacts
The recommendations are developed and reviewed with input from patients panel members with lived and living experiences, ensuring that considerations around access, feasibility and acceptability of the interventions are addressed. This ensures that health equity is integral to the recommendation development and implementation process.
SGBA Plus data collection plan
As part of the solicitation process, projects were required to integrate SGBA Plus considerations into their proposals, which were assessed during the screening process, and were encouraged to collect and use available disaggregated data on relevant variables. Recipients also provide internal progress reports related to SGBA Plus aspects, such as sex, gender, age, Indigenous identity, disability, urban vs. rural status, level of education, employment status, general health status, primary language spoken and ethnicity. Summaries of projects are posted online by PHAC.
Program name: Evidence for Health Promotion, and Chronic Disease and Injury Prevention – Integrated Strategy for Healthy Living and Chronic Disease – Enhanced Surveillance for Chronic Disease
Program goals: The Enhanced Surveillance for Chronic Disease Program aims is to build surveillance capacity and fill data gaps in priority areas. Under the terms of contribution agreements established, recipients will be responsible for submitting progress reports semi-annually, until they close.
Target population: Key populations including 2SLGBTQI+, people living in situations of vulnerability, racialized people, older adults (i.e., 65 and over), rural and remote populations, populations living with low socio-economic status and other populations where data are limited.
Third group: broadly gender-balanced
Third group: no significant distributional impacts
Second group: no significant intergenerational impacts or impacts on generations between youths and seniors
Specific demographic group(s) to be monitored
Persistent gaps remain in chronic disease and/or risk factor surveillance related to demographic groups, which are not effectively captured through current surveys or traditional methodologies.
The program has placed a focus on key populations in its solicitations, including Indigenous populations, institutionalized populations, 2SLGBTQI+ communities, rural and remote populations, racialized groups, recent immigrants/new Canadians and other populations where evidence is lacking for specific topics (e.g., children, youth, older adults, etc.).
Key program impacts on gender and diversity
As the seven funded projects have only completed their first year of implementation, surveillance results are not yet available. Preliminary findings may begin to emerge in 2025–26, with final results expected in 2026–27.
In the meantime, the table below highlights the key populations being examined across the projects. This early insight helps illustrate the diversity of focus areas and the potential value of the data being generated.
Recipient Organization
Key Populations being Surveyed
British Columbia Centre for Disease Control

SGBA Plus data collection plan
As part of the solicitation processes, funded organizations integrated SGBA Plus considerations into their projects. Some examples of information being captured for analysis in this program include sex, gender, age, Indigenous identity, disability, urban vs. rural status, level of education, employment status, general health status, including diagnosed mental health conditions, race, and immigration status.
Program name: Health Promotion – Community Action Program for Children (CAPC) and Canada Prenatal Nutrition Program (CPNP)
Program goals: The CAPC and CPNP provide funding to community groups to support pregnant women and people, children aged 0 to 6 and their parents and guardians who experience challenges may that place their health at risk.
Funded projects provide stigma-free, culturally safe and inclusive environments, applying health equity and social determinants of health-based approaches to encourage healthy pregnancies, positive parenting and healthy child development in populations facing health equity barriers.
Target population: Pregnant women and people, children (aged 0 to 6) and their families who face challenges that may put their health at risk. These conditions may include low socioeconomic status, teenage pregnancy or parenthood, social or geographic isolation, recent arrival to Canada and situations of violence or neglect. Special emphasis is placed on the inclusion of Indigenous families living in urban and rural communities.
Fourth group: 60% to 79% women
First group: strongly benefits individuals living on low-income (strongly progressive)
First group: primarily benefits youth, children or future generations
Specific demographic group(s) to be monitored
CAPC and CPNP directly benefit pregnant women and people, children aged 0 to 6 and their parents and guardians who experience health related risks. and face increased barriers in accessing supports and programs due to systemic inequalities. Funded projects aim to decrease barriers to participation by providing stigma-free, culturally appropriate and inclusive environments.
Key program impacts on gender and diversity
In 2025, an online survey was conducted over a 6-week period to examine outcomes experienced by participants in CAPC. Based on those who responded to the survey (n=3,713) the results were disaggregated by gender (3,407 respondents stated they were female, 260 stated they were male and 7 chose the "another gender" response category).
Results disaggregated by gender are as follows:
- 87% of female respondents and 89% of male respondents reported gaining knowledge and skills because of attending the program;
- 83% of female respondents and 84% of male respondents reported improved health behaviours because of attending the program;
- 88% of female respondents and 90% of male respondents reported experiencing improved protective factors because of the program; and
- 88% of female respondents and 86% of male respondents reported that their health and wellbeing improved because of attending the program.
Additional relevant program data includes:
- Of those CAPC program participants who responded to an online survey collected during a 6-week period in 2025: 56% reported having a total household income of $60,800 or less, 25% were lone parents or guardians, 25% were recent immigrants, 10% identified as First Nations, 4% identified as Métis, 0.2% identified as Inuit, 9% had less than a high school education and 0.5% were teenage parents.
- Of the CPNP program participants who responded to an online survey collected during a 6-week period in 2025: 60% reported having a total household income of $60,800 or less, 22% were lone parents or guardians, 34% were recent immigrants, 14% identified as First Nations, 5% identified as Métis and 1% identified as Inuit, 8% had less than a high school education and 2% of respondents were teenage parents.
% of CAPC and CPNP projects offering programs by language
74% of CAPC and 95% of CPNP projects offer programs in English 58% of CAPC projects and 28% of CPNP projects offer programs in French 8% of CAPC and 9% of CPNP projects offer programs in Indigenous languages 18% of CAPC and 19% CPNP projects offer programs in other languages
74% of CAPC and 95% of CPNP projects offer programs in English
58% of CAPC projects and 28% of CPNP projects offer programs in French
8% of CAPC and 9% of CPNP projects offer programs in Indigenous languages
18% of CAPC and 19% CPNP projects offer programs in other languages
Annual Reporting Tool (2022–23)
% of CAPC and CPNP projects delivering programming in rural, remote or isolated areas
31% of CAPC and 41% of CPNP deliver –programming in rural areas, 5% of CAPC and 5% of CPNP offer programming in remote areas, and 3% of CAPC and 6% of CPNP offer programming in isolated areas.
Annual Reporting Tool (2022–23)
Other key program impacts
In 2022–23, approximately 87% of CAPC and 95% of CPNP projects facilitated access to programs by offering support services such as respite, transportation and translation and interpretation.
Supplementary information sources
Evaluation of PHAC's CAPC and CPNP 2015–16 to 2019–20.
SGBA Plus data collection plan
The program is exploring ways to gather participant-level socio-demographic and outcome information in the future and will consider options for disaggregation.
Program name: Health Promotion – Dementia Community Investment (DCI)
Program goals: Projects funded through the DCI are required to report on the populations reached that have a higher risk of developing dementia and/or face barriers to equitable care and support to improve the understanding of the effectiveness of interventions. The DCI applies SGBA Plus and integrates health equity considerations in its solicitation processes where applicable (e.g., reporting on data disaggregated by sociodemographic and socioeconomic factors). Applicants of DCI solicitation processes are also asked to incorporate health equity and cultural sensitivity considerations in their proposed projects.
Target population: People living with dementia, family or friend caregivers, public, older adults, Indigenous Peoples, individuals and/or families living on low-income, 2SLGBTQI+ communities, racialized people, official language minority communities, rural or remote communities, and people living with disabilities.
Fifth group: predominantly women (80% or more women)
The DCI does not collect enough data on income level to be able to report on the distribution of benefits
Third group: primarily benefits seniors or the baby boom generation
Specific demographic group(s) to be monitored
The DCI supports projects that aim to improve the quality of life of people living with dementia, people who are at risk of developing dementia and family or friend caregivers.
Key program impacts on gender and diversity
Observed results Footnote *
% of community-based intervention projects that identify Indigenous communities as a specific target population
% of community-based intervention projects that identify 2SLGBTQI+ as a specific target population
Footnote * 2023–24 or most recent Return to footnote * referrer
2023–24 or most recent
Return to footnote * referrer
SGBA Plus data collection plan
Projects funded through the DCI are asked to consider various intersecting factors including sex, gender, race, and others, into their proposals and are encouraged to report on these considerations in their annual reporting to PHAC when applicable. In 2024–25, PHAC held regular meetings with recipients over the course of the year to support relationship building, strengthen collaboration, and share relevant information. Recipients also submitted semi-annual progress reports, with final reports due at project completion to outline outcomes achieved through the investment. The DCI continues to work to improve disaggregated data at the project level by updating project reporting templates and instructions.
Program name: Health Promotion – Distress Line Equity Fund
Program goals: The Distress Line Equity Fund supports organizations providing crisis and distress line services, particularly for populations with higher suicide rates, such as Indigenous Peoples and members of 2SLGBTQI+ communities.
Target population: General population, people from equity-deserving populations.
Third group: broadly gender-balanced
Information not available
Second group: no significant intergenerational impacts or impacts on generations between youths and seniors
Specific demographic group(s) to be monitored
Projects launched under the Distress Line Equity Fund helped to increase the capacity and competency of distress lines and centres to provide support and be more responsive to the needs and experiences of equity-deserving populations across Canada who are seeking out suicide or emotional crisis services, regardless of their cultural, social or economic status.
Key program impacts on gender and diversity
Most projects were completed March 31, 2025 and will identify impacts through their final reports. The reports will support impact assessments completed by PHAC.
SGBA Plus data collection plan
Through the solicitation process, applicants were required to integrate SGBA Plus considerations into their proposals, which were assessed during the screening process. Specifically, they were asked to describe how the project will fill gaps in diversity, equity and inclusion within their distress line to better meet the needs of those they serve. Upon completion of their project, funding recipients were asked to provide both quantitative and qualitative data on how their project met their equity, diversity, and inclusion goals.
Program name: Health Promotion – Mental Health Promotion Innovation Fund (MHP-IF)
Program goals: The MHP-IF supports diverse community-based interventions that bolster protective factors for the mental health of young Canadians, families and communities. The program actively seeks to remove barriers to health equity through strong partnerships, reflexive practice, learning, knowledge exchange and systems change.
Specific efforts to reduce mental health inequalities through action on systemic barriers and underlying determinants of health such as gender identity, geographic location and culture are mandatory requirements of funded projects. Funded initiatives must consider such inequalities and/or improve health equity at various stages of the intervention, including design and adaptation, implementation, scale and evaluation.
Target population: Children, youth, young adults, parents and guardians, Indigenous Peoples, individuals and/or families living on low-income and 2SLGBTQI+ communities.
Third group: broadly gender-balanced
Second group: somewhat benefits individuals living on low-income (somewhat progressive)
First group: primarily benefits youth, children or future generations
Specific demographic group(s) to be monitored
The MHP-IF projects are reaching priority groups disproportionately experiencing to mental health inequities, such as: First Nations, Inuit and Métis, 2SLGBTQI+, newcomers and refugees and people with other socioeconomic risk factors. Outcomes are not yet available by specific demographic groups.
Key program impacts on gender and diversity
Observed results Footnote *
% of intervention research projects focused on reaching indigenous children, youth and families
Phase 2 Annual Report
% of intervention research projects focused on reaching 2SLGBTQI+ children, youth and their families.
Phase 2 Annual Report
% of intervention research projects focused on reaching children, youth and families who are visible minorities
Phase 2 Annual Report
% of intervention research projects focused on reaching children, youth and families who are official language minorities
Phase 2 Annual Report
% of intervention research projects focused on reaching children, youth and families who are newcomers (including refugees)
Phase 2 Annual Report
Footnote * 2022–23 or most recent Return to footnote * referrer
2022–23 or most recent
Return to footnote * referrer
Other key program impacts
MHP-IF projects used several approaches to address health equity though the implementation and evaluation of their interventions. These include engaging people with lived and living experience, providing access to travel and meal subsidies, using diverse evaluation methods (e.g., Two-eyed seeing, Inuit storytelling, and journaling), providing resources in numerous languages, and learning about the priorities, values, experiences, strengths and perspectives of communities.
SGBA Plus data collection plan
The MHP-IF developed, piloted, and shared the Health Equity Indicator Tool (HEIT) piloted in 2023 to support funded projects reporting on health equity that builds on existing PHAC commitments to understand, measure, and reduce inequities in health. Components of the HEIT have been integrated into annual reporting and ca be used by identifying components of an intervention that address health equity and possible gaps by strengthening capacity to integrate, monitor and assess impacts on health equity. This exercise continues to contribute to building a common set of health equity measures that all projects can reference in program reporting tools.
Program name: Health Promotion – 9-8-8: Suicide Crisis Helpline
Program goals: The goal of the 9-8-8 Suicide Crisis Helpline is to provide people across Canada with access to free, bilingual, 24/7 crisis support from trained responders, by phone call or text through an easy to remember three-digit number: 9-8-8. An Equity Working Group was created to help inform the implementation of 9-8-8.
Target population: All Canadians, as well as those at higher risk of suicide, including men and boys, Indigenous Peoples, 2SLGBTQI+ people, and those from certain occupational groups, such as public safety personnel.
Third group: broadly gender-balanced
Information not available
Second group: no significant intergenerational impacts or impacts on generations between youths and seniors
Specific demographic group(s) to be monitored
The target group for the 9-8-8: Suicide Crisis Helpline is "all Canadians". Suicide can affect anyone in Canada regardless of their age, background, gender or socioeconomic status.
Key program impacts on gender and diversity
Observed results Footnote 1
Total number of answered interactions
Monthly and annual reports from the funding recipient
Total number of answered interactions
Number of answered interactions by modality
Voice: 221,634 Text: 154,458
Monthly and annual reports from the funding recipient
Number of answered interactions by modality
Other key program impacts
Some of the activities that took place in 2024–25 to support diverse populations include:
- People calling and texting 9-8-8 are presented with the option to select English or French. In some parts of the country, young people may chose to speak with youth-specific support, such as Kids Help Phone, and Indigenous callers can chose to be connected to the Hope for Wellness Helpline. The Hope for Wellness Helpline is a national service that is funded by Indigenous Services Canada and available to all First Nations, Inuit and Métis people across Canada, 24 hours a day, 7 days a week, offering support in English and in French, as well as Cree, Ojibwe (Anishinaabemowin), and Inuktitut upon request.
- CAMH's "Coping for All" project provided distress centres in Canada with curated resources to assist in meeting the diverse needs of priority and populations living in situation of vulnerability. This work has included engagement and an environmental scan, an equity-informed needs assessment, and resource collection and development.
- A post-interaction survey is under development in collaboration with stakeholders with expertise in evaluating health equity to pilot collecting demographic data from a sample of 9-8-8 callers. Survey testing and stakeholder feedback will determine if a survey is an appropriate tool for evaluating user experience by population to plan ongoing equity-informed quality improvement.
- Content was developed and shared to mark key external campaign days and events, including the Trans Day of Visibility, World Mental Health Day, International Women's Day and the International Day for the Elimination of Racial Discrimination.
SGBA Plus data collection plan
While some demographic information is collected through calls and text) and from automated technology, such as language selection (i.e., selecting 1 for English and 2 for French), it is limited to what information callers provide during the interaction as demographic data is not asked of callers. Province or territory may also be determined based on the area code from which the call or text originates, however, with the portability of cellphones, this data may not be fully representative of the location of people reaching out.
Program name: Health Promotion – Preventing Family Violence Program (previously referred to as the Preventing Gender-Based Violence: The Health Perspective and the Preventing and Addressing Family Violence: The Health Perspective investments)
Program goals: The Preventing Family Violence Program invests in the delivery and testing of health promotion programs and interventions that:
- Prevent family violence;
- Equip health and social service professionals to respond safely and effectively to family violence; and
- Improve health outcomes for survivors of family violence.
SGBA Plus considerations are built into solicitation implementation, roll-out and design. For example, SGBA Plus considerations were integrated into new solicitation documents, such as funding request forms, as well as into project reporting templates for existing projects.
Target population: Children, youth, families, survivors, public, older adults, Indigenous Peoples, individuals and families living on low-income and 2SLGBTQI+ communities.
Participants: Third group: broadly gender-balanced Professionals: Fifth group: 80+% women (partial data available).
Participants: Third group: broadly gender-balanced
Professionals: Fifth group: 80+% women (partial data available).
Information not available.
Information not available.
Specific demographic group(s) to be monitored
The Preventing Family Violence Program is designed to address gendered forms of violence that primarily affect women, girls, children and gender-diverse youth. Projects that work with youth report more participants with a non-binary gender. Adults form the largest participant group for project participants in 2025–26 as they represent survivors, parents and caregivers, as well as professionals and service providers. It is anticipated that the number of youth participants will increase in upcoming years as the youth dating violence prevention projects begin program implementation.
Key program impacts on gender and diversity
The 2024–25 data shows that the Preventing Family Violence Program was particularly successful in reaching newcomers and visible minorities, Indigenous and 2SLGBTQI+ participants. Some projects reported on characteristics like living on a low income, living in a rural area, having a disability, being a single parent, and being part of an official language minority community. Due to the variability in methods and reporting, it is not possible to provide detailed analyses based on these data.

Other key program impacts
Dedicated funding streams were created to prioritize equity-deserving groups in the youth dating violence prevention open call for proposals. Consequently, 9 projects tailored to key populations at greater risk of experiencing violence were funded, including newcomer communities, 2SLGBTQI+ youth, people with disabilities and Black and other racialized Canadians. Another 3 projects tailored to the needs of Indigenous populations were also funded. There was also a focus on upstream interventions to support a public health approach to child welfare, targeting families at-risk or involved with child protection services.
The call for proposals launched in March 2025 to prevent and address child maltreatment aims to give special consideration to proposals for projects that are designed to meet the needs of groups who face barriers to accessing supports, groups who face multiple and intersecting forms of discrimination, and groups who are disproportionately affected by child maltreatment. These include but are not limited to:
- populations in remote or northern areas
- First Nations, Inuit and Métis
- persons with disabilities
- newcomer, immigrant and/or refugee families
- fathers/male caregivers
- official language minority communities
- families facing multiple complex challenges (for example, poverty, substance use, risk of child welfare system involvement, etc.)
SGBA Plus data collection plan
To improve the programs' ability to report disaggregated data, changes were made in early 2023 to the annual report templates to encourage projects to report their disaggregated reach numbers if available.
There are no plans to disaggregate mandatory indicators. It is not feasible to disaggregate these indicators, since they are aggregates of all projects' input, with a wide variety of measurements, target groups, question wording and methodology.

### Core Responsibility 2: Infectious disease prevention and control
Program name: Communicable Disease and Infection Control
Program goals: The purpose of the program is to prevent and control the spread of infectious disease in communities and healthcare settings. The program is striving to achieve this goal through collaboration with domestic and international stakeholders to share knowledge, tools and resources to support public health front-line efforts, including tailored community-based interventions, to prevent, control and reduce community and healthcare-associated infections. It promotes and coordinates a pan-Canadian approach to disaggregated data collection to help identify key populations and locations to provide evidence-informed public health measures guidance, address barriers to healthcare services related to STBBI and the reduction of harm, stigma and discrimination toward diverse populations disproportionally affected by STBBI, including people living with HIV or hepatitis C.
Target population: Health care professionals, public, provincial and territorial governments and non-governmental organizations.
Third group: broadly gender-balanced
Third group: no significant distributional impacts
Second group: no significant intergenerational impacts or impacts on generations between youths and seniors
Specific demographic group(s) to be monitored
This program benefits Canadians.
Key program impacts on gender and diversity
Led by PHAC, the HIV and Hepatitis C Community Action Fund (CAF) and Harm Reduction Fund (HRF) support time-limited projects from three to five years across Canada to address HIV, hepatitis C and other sexually transmitted and blood-borne infections (STBBI), particularly among people who share injection and inhalation drug-use equipment. These programs are grounded in equity and inclusion, with a strong focus on gender and diversity. Projects receiving funding were reviewed by a diverse committee comprised of individuals from key populations such as people living with HIV, 2SLGBTQI+ individuals, First Nations, Inuit and Métis, and women, alongside researchers and front-line workers. Review committees were structured to ensure representation across region, gender, age, population group, expertise and lived experience. The projects which received funding were designed to recognize intersecting identities and structural barriers for key populations, including gender-based and racial inequities, social determinants of health and the disproportionate impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on marginalized communities. This inclusive approach supports tailored interventions that enhance the program's overall impact in reducing health inequities.
PHAC continues to engage with key representatives, partners and stakeholders throughout the span of project to ensure that surveillance data and findings are appropriately contextualized in a culturally relevant and safe manner.
Community-based organizations are particularly well positioned to reach key populations because of existing relationships with the populations they serve, allowing for a greater likelihood that people would have the necessary supports and linkages to care following the screening result. The information shared with program officers through the consultation process, as well as and findings from surveys accentuate the need to apply an equity lens and develop public health interventions which address barriers to STBBI-related and other services rooted in social determinants of health.
Supplementary information sources
The HIV in Canada Surveillance Report to December 31, 2022 , published by PHAC in July 2024, presents national epidemiological trends on HIV diagnoses in Canada by geographic region, age at diagnosis, sex, race and/or ethnicity and exposure category between 2013 and 2022.
SGBA Plus data collection plan
In 2024–25, data disaggregated by sociodemographic and socioeconomic factors (e.g., age, disability inclusion, economic status, education, gender, race, ethnicity, religion and sex) and their intersectionality were analyzed and considered in order to improve data collection and achieve more equitable and inclusive outcomes for all peoples and communities within Canada, particularly for Indigenous peoples, racialized people and marginalized populations. In support of the Pan-Canadian Health Data Strategy, PHAC worked closely with federal, provincial and territorial stakeholders, Indigenous groups and community partners to develop new indicators for the purpose of improving the quality and availability of disaggregated data to ensure that policy decisions benefit all communities. The development of indicators and targets is critical for the measurement and reporting of progress against the strategic goals of the Pan-Canadian Framework for Action and aligned with global commitments to work toward reducing the health impacts of STBBI in Canada.
PHAC undertook the process of reviewing and renewing the National HIV/AIDS Surveillance System (HASS) with the goal of better meeting evidence needs. In accordance with Canada's Anti-Racism Strategy, a collaborative effort with community members led to the establishment of a Black Expert Working Group, which will provide advice to HASS and contribute to the co-development and implementation of strategies to improve the completeness of the race and/or ethnicity variable, which will in turn inform the development of better research evidence and more appropriately tailored prevention programs.
As we build capacity and foster collaboration and cohesiveness, PHAC continues to strengthen its surveillance activities as well as its engagement and knowledge mobilization activities to better capture the realities of the diverse population groups in Canada and support healthcare providers as well as community-based organizations in delivering services that are stigma-free, culturally safe and inclusive.
Program name: Foodborne and Zoonotic Disease
Program goals: The Infectious Disease and Climate Change (IDCC) Program and Fund focuses on preparing and protecting Canadians from climate-sensitive infectious diseases that are zoonotic, food-borne and water-borne. The program and fund incorporate SGBA Plus considerations into program implementation, roll-out and design. For example, SGBA Plus considerations were integrated into new solicitation documents (e.g., funding application forms, application guides, and evaluation forms, as well as the integration of SGBA Plus metrics into new project reporting templates.
Target population: Health care professionals, public, provincial and territorial governments
Third group: broadly gender-balanced
Third group: no significant distributional impacts
Second group: no significant intergenerational impacts or impacts on generations between youths and seniors
Specific demographic group(s) to be monitored
All Canadians, including youth and specific regions or sectors of the economy:
- Remote regions, rural areas, urban areas, specific provinces or territories; and

Other key program impacts
The IDCC Fund continues to invest in projects which support surveillance and monitoring, education for health professionals and activities that raise public awareness related to climate-sensitive infectious diseases in Canada. In 2024–25, the 13 projects which were underway continued to advance activities that align with SGBA Plus considerations. Project goals included:
- increasing participation in schools to engage children and youth in citizen science activities and raise awareness about infectious diseases, climate change and health through class presentations and the development of educational resources;
- engaging children, youth and families in citizen science such as tick and mosquito collection activities; and
- engaging provincial immigration services to raise awareness among immigrant and refugee populations.
Ongoing engagement of Indigenous communities to build capacity and establish surveillance and monitoring strategies, and to develop culturally relevant education, training and awareness materials related to vector-borne diseases.
SGBA Plus data collection plan
The IDCC Program incorporates SGBA Plus considerations into program implementation, roll-out and design, including funding application forms, guides, evaluation forms and performance measurement and reporting templates. In 2024–25, the IDCC Fund incorporated a suite of questions in its annual and final reporting and performance measurement requirements seeking information regarding which equity deserving populations were prioritized, including how and by which method, and by what means systemic barriers to health equity related to infectious diseases, climate change and the impact on human health were identified and reduced.
The IDCC Program continues to engage with stakeholders and interested parties to further refine and apply knowledge gained to improve the integration of SGBA Plus metrics into program delivery. The IDCC Program will also continue to disseminate resources that support recipients in incorporating SGBA Plus considerations into projects to advance health equity.
Program name: Laboratory Science Leadership and Services
Program goals: This program's objective is to provide Canadians with the scientific readiness to respond to infectious disease threats. To that end, a governance mechanism was established to provide accountability on and oversight of SGBA Plus application.
Target population: Scientific researchers, health care professionals, provincial and territorial governments.
Third group: broadly gender-balanced
Third group: no significant distributional impacts
Second group: no significant intergenerational impacts or impacts on generations between youths and seniors
Specific demographic group(s) to be monitored
Laboratory Science Leadership and Services will benefit all Canadians.
Key program impacts on gender and diversity
In general, laboratory services involve testing samples from clients who were not required to submit patient information and metrics, such as gender, for privacy reasons. Where possible, research and testing activities considered SGBA Plus on a project-by-project basis.
Positive impacts continued to be realized in 2024–25 through providing equitable access to laboratory testing, particularly through the expansion of testing capacity in remote and dispersed populations across Canada with limited access to testing facilities. The National Microbiology Laboratory (NML) supported community-led diagnostic capacity in Northern, Remote and Isolated (NRI) communities by maintaining quality oversight for NRI sites that conduct diagnostic testing, evaluating deployable testing technologies, and delivering training sessions, consequently enabling decentralized testing and supporting principles of self-determination. Notable progress included support for less invasive methods of diagnosis for enteric diseases, which disproportionately impact rural, remote and Indigenous communities, contributing to improved treatment outcomes for all Canadians regardless of socioeconomic status or geographic location. Further positive impacts are also being realized through various consultations with local public health authorities, affected communities and other informed stakeholders. Finally, mathematical quantitative modellers in NML continued to integrate equity guidelines into synthesis research to highlight knowledge gaps and incorporate considerations of demographic representation into infectious disease forecasting, thereby reflecting the integration of SGBA Plus and equity-informed analysis.
SGBA Plus data collection plan
NML regularly receives samples for diagnosis from the provinces and territories (PTs). Without government-to-government information sharing agreements in place, there is no standardization of information that NML can request. The branch does not collect information directly from individuals, and therefore, does not control the information. Furthermore, most PTs have their own information governance frameworks which in some cases preclude them from sharing these disaggregated data.
However, to support the application of SGBA Plus and generation of knowledge and evidence disaggregated by sociodemographic and socioeconomic factors, NML is currently implementing an internal SGBA Plus strategy, which includes increasing the branch's SGBA Plus capacity and encouraging employees to consider SGBA Plus implications on a project-by-project basis.
Program name: Vaccination – Immunization Partnership Fund (IPF)
Program goals: The purpose of the IPF is to support vaccine confidence and uptake among Canadians, particularly those in underserved communities, through initiatives that are evidence-informed, culturally safe, equity-promoting and tailored to reflect the historic and present-day drivers of vaccine hesitancy.
Target population: All Canadians, with specific focus on underserved groups such as: Indigenous Peoples, Black Canadians, ethnic, linguistic, cultural and religious minorities, individuals with chronic health concerns and/or disabilities, members of the 2SLGBTQI+ community, parents, guardians and families, pregnant women and people, children and youth, older adults, newcomers, people experiencing homelessness, people who use drugs and individuals of particular socioeconomic status.
Third group: broadly gender-balanced
Second group: somewhat benefits individuals living on low-income (somewhat progressive)
First group: primarily benefits youth, children or future generations
Specific demographic group(s) to be monitored
Groups that may benefit directly from IPF initiatives include Black Canadians, Indigenous Peoples, ethnic, linguistic, cultural and religious minorities, individuals with chronic health concerns and or disabilities, members of the 2SLGBTQI+ community, parents, guardians and families, pregnant women and people, children and youth, older adults, newcomers, people experiencing homelessness, people who use drugs and individuals of socioeconomic status. Most IPF initiatives offer a variety of low-barrier approaches to facilitate access and participation by their intended audience.
Indirectly, all individuals in Canada may benefit from IPF initiatives, particularly those hosted by organizations with mandates that are national in scope. Individuals with disabilities may experience barriers to accessing or participating in IPF initiatives that are tailored for other key populations.
It is not anticipated that any group will experience negative impacts due to IPF initiatives.

Other key program impacts
In 2024–25, as in the previous reporting period the IPF continued to support broader vaccine confidence and uptake activities to address the decrease in coverage rates for routine vaccinations in Canada during the pandemic. Funding recipients continued to prioritize the development of tailored, multifaced interventions at the local, regional and national level to build confidence and capacity among health care providers as vaccinators, to support underserved communities in achieving vaccine equity and to promote evidence-informed vaccination communication while building science, health, and digital literacy among Canadians.
These gender-balanced, equitable, and inclusive initiatives aimed to provide direct benefits to people disproportionately impacted by vaccine preventable illnesses such as Black, Indigenous Peoples and racialized populations in Canada, as well as priority populations including healthcare providers, older adults, children and youth, pregnant women and people, members of the 2SLGBTQI+ community and other disproportionately impacted populations.
IPF funding recipients continued to employ a variety of equity promoting approaches to respond to the intersectional needs of their intended audiences, such as:
- Inclusive and culturally relevant spaces and resources, including representative project staff;
- Community engagement and peer-led models to build relationships and trust;
- Addressing systemic barriers that hinder access to information and/or health services;
- Improving accessibility and convenience by using mobile and pop-up clinics;
- Building trust and reducing stigma associated with certain health conditions or experiences;
- Educating and empowering individuals to make informed decisions; and
- Collaborating through the development of strong community partnerships and knowledge sharing events.
SGBA Plus data collection plan
Throughout 2024–25, the IPF collated and analyzed data from multiple data sources to monitor the program's progress toward achieving planned objectives and expected outcomes. Data collection points include:
- Mid-Year Progress Reports for the April 1, 2024 to October 31, 2024 and the November 1, 2024 to March 31, 2025 reporting periods respectively. Progress reports solicit updates to project activities, as well as a summary of successes to date. Recipients are encouraged to collect and report data disaggregated by sociodemographic and socioeconomic factors, when possible.
- Annual Performance Measurement Reports for the April 1, 2024 to March 31, 2025 reporting period. The annual performance measurement tool collects comprehensive qualitative and quantitative data to describe the outputs and outcomes achieved by funding recipients.

### Core Responsibility 3: Health security
Program name: Biosecurity
Program goals: The Biosecurity program administers and enforces the Human Pathogens and Toxins Act and Regulations (HPTA/R) and select sections of the Health of Animals Act and Regulations (HAA/R). The program and regulatory framework are designed to prevent accidents and incidents with dangerous pathogens and biological toxins, as well as deter the development of biological weapons.
Target population: All Canadians, science and technology industry, health care and social assistance sectors, colleges and universities.
Third group: broadly gender-balanced
Third group: no significant distributional impacts
Second group: no significant intergenerational impacts or impacts on generations between youths and seniors
Specific demographic group(s) to be monitored
All Canadians benefit from this program, including those at higher risk for adverse health outcomes during public health emergencies.
Key program impacts on gender and diversity
The program provides regulated organizations and facilities with a range of guidance, resources and tools to support them in identifying and mitigating biosafety and biosecurity risks as well as fulfilling their obligations under the Human Pathogens and Toxins Act (HPTA) and Human Pathogens and Toxins Regulations (HPTR). To that end, when developing resources that describe the hazardous properties of a human pathogen and/or provide guidance on how to work safely with these agents in a laboratory setting, PHAC identifies if certain populations (e.g., pregnant women and people, older adults) are at increased risk to take necessary precautions.
SGBA Plus data collection plan
Though the collection of disaggregated data to support the application of SGBA Plus on impacts is limited due to the nature and scope of work, the program will continue to routinely incorporate SGBA Plus considerations into its activities, including the development of guidance, resources and tools. However, in recognition of the changing Canadian biomanufacturing and life sciences footprint, PHAC will undertake an assessment of SGBA Plus relevant factors in facilities and organizations handling human pathogens and toxins planned for 2025–26. Additionally, as part of the planned legislative renewal of the HPTA and HPTR, PHAC will undertake an assessment of the social and economic impacts of proposed amendments on diverse groups of Canadians.
Program name: Border and Travel Health
Program goals: This program protects the travelling public by informing travellers how to protect themselves from travel-related public health risks, working with the conveyance industry to protect against risks associated with water, food and sanitation. This program administers and enforces the Quarantine Act working with border partners to limit the introduction and spread of public health risks to protect Canada's health care system and meet International Health Regulation requirements. In general, the program's service delivery approach is aimed at the whole targeted population, and where possible, adapted to specific groups such as women, older adults, children, pregnant women and pregnant people to mitigate disparate impacts.
Target population: All Canadians, residents of Canada and travellers to Canada.
Third group: broadly gender-balanced
Third group: no significant distributional impacts
Second group: no significant intergenerational impacts or impacts on generations between youths and seniors
Specific demographic group(s) to be monitored
All Canadians, residents of Canada and travellers to Canada benefit from this program. However, there is greater benefit for those in situations of vulnerability such as older adults, children and pregnant women and pregnant people.
Key program impacts on gender and diversity
The Border and Travel Health Program aims to have a broad impact on all sections of the targeted population as public health risks associated with water, food and sanitation, and the spread of communicable diseases may impact all Canadians, as well as travellers to Canada. The program includes key SGBA Plus considerations where possible, and considerations to improve protections for priority populations who may be disproportionately impacted by public health risks.
SGBA Plus data collection plan
With respect to health policy advice related to borders and travel, the program is significantly reliant on publicly available data and information disaggregated by sociodemographic and socioeconomic factors, such as from Statistics Canada and other credible sources.
The program uses online intercept surveys to collect gendered data relating to Canadians travelling internationally who visit the Government of Canada's travel.gc.ca website seeking health advice. This l helps inform the development of messaging and products identified in the Office of Travel Health's three-year Promotions and Outreach Strategy (2023-2026).
Certain clients for border and travel health services are corporate entities and the data collected as part of the delivery of these services cannot be disaggregated at this time. The program will continue to explore potential ways to collect data disaggregated by socio-demographic and socio-economic factors, where feasible, through the evolution of supporting data systems that were put in place during the COVID-19 pandemic to inform and integrate SGBA Plus and health equity considerations.
Program name: Emergency Preparedness and Response
Program goals: This program is responsible for strengthening Canada's capacity to prevent, mitigate, prepare for and respond to public health events and emergencies. The program recognises that disasters, events and emergencies have the potential to exacerbate health inequities and disproportionately impact some people over others. The program exists for all Canadians. However, where possible, the diverse needs of priority populations (i.e., men and women, older adults, Indigenous Peoples and individuals living in vulnerable situations) have been considered and built into the service delivery while applying a health equity lens.
Target population: All Canadians, public health and emergency management professionals working at PHAC and provincial and territorial governments.
Third group: broadly gender-balanced
Third group: no significant distributional impacts
Second group: no significant intergenerational impacts or impacts on generations between youths and seniors
Specific demographic group(s) to be monitored
All Canadians benefit from this program including those who are disproportionately impacted such as older adults, people who are immunocompromised, individuals living on low-income, 2SLGBTQI+ people, racialized people or remote Indigenous communities.
Key program impacts on gender and diversity
The Emergency Preparedness and Response Program aims to have a broad impact on all sections of the population as events and emergencies impact all Canadians. The program includes key SGBA Plus considerations where possible, such as to inform PHAC's work to support medical supply readiness in Canada, including for the procurement of medical supplies and equipment that consider end-user preferences and needs. For example, the National Emergency Strategic Stockpile (NESS) acquires products in various sizes and considers alternatives for certain populations (e.g., pregnant women and people, people with underlying health conditions) where there may be contraindications to certain medical countermeasures (e.g., vaccines and therapeutics), requiring alternative forms of medication and medical products.
The training and development team weaves health equity, reconciliation and anti-oppression themes throughout their trainings. Courses with a focus on building cultural humility and health equity competencies are offered on a biannual basis. This year, training topics included Applied Learning on 2SLGBTQI+ Epidemiology, which was offered to public health professionals.
SGBA Plus data collection plan
Though the collection of disaggregated data to support SGBA Plus on impacts is limited due to the nature and scope of work, the program will continue to incorporate SGBA Plus considerations into its activities, including emergency response planning, training activities and medical asset procurement. For example, its training programs will continue to increase the emphasis placed on integrating a health equity lens in emergency management and applying principles of SGBA Plus during a public health response.
